coredata.dc:title: A new approach to modeling and simulation of the nonlinear, fractional behavior of Li-ion battery cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Tappeiner, Lukas
Oldenburger, Marc
Bedürftig, Benjamin
Woittennek, Frank
coredata.dc:description: Two nonlinear infinite dimensional models of a Lithium-ion battery are introduced. The proposed models interpolate linear fractional models which are obtained from a widely spread equivalent circuit model. The linear models are parameterized by means of so-called dynamic impedance measurements, i.e. impedance measurements in several operating points. In each operating point, the fractional input output behavior is recovered by an infinite dimensional state space description. For numerical implementation the introduced models require for an appropriate approximation. Therefore, a Krylov subspace method and a finite element approach are proposed. The nonlinear lumped parameter models are validated on the basis of experimental data.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fractional transfer function
Simulation
Impedance spectrum
Nonlinear model
Li-ion battery

Finite dimensional approximation of the nECM
Finite dimensional approximation of the nSSM

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The Nyquist representation of the DEIS of a battery is shown in (a). In (b) the ECM which is used to describe the measurements is shown.
Parameters of the nonlinear battery models and validation of the small-signal dynamics.

Validation of the battery models at
70
% SOC, ε denotes the error referred to the measurement.

Validation of the battery models at
90
% SOC, ε denotes the error referred to the measurement.

head.ce:title: A new approach to modeling and simulation of the nonlinear, fractional behavior of Li-ion battery cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Automation and Control Engineering, UMIT – University for Health Sciences, Medical Informatics and Technology, Eduard Wallnöfer Zentrum 1, Hall in Tirol, Austria

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Automation and Control Engineering, UMIT – University for Health Sciences, Medical Informatics and Technology
sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: Eduard Wallnöfer Zentrum 1, Hall in Tirol

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Automation and Control Engineering, UMIT – University for Health Sciences, Medical Informatics and Technology, Eduard Wallnfer Zentrum 1, Hall in Tirol, Austria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Mercedes-Benz AG, Wilhelm-Runge Str. 11, 89081 Ulm, Germany

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory for Systems Theory and Automatic Control, Otto von Guericke University Magdeburg, Universitätsplatz 2, 39106 Magdeburg, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory for Systems Theory and Automatic Control, Otto von Guericke University Magdeburg

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory for Systems Theory and Automatic Control, Otto von Guericke University Magdeburg, 2, 39106 Magdeburg, Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two nonlinear infinite dimensional models of a Lithium-ion battery are introduced. The proposed models interpolate linear fractional models which are obtained from a widely spread equivalent circuit model. The linear models are parameterized by means of so-called dynamic impedance measurements, i.e. impedance measurements in several operating points. In each operating point, the fractional input output behavior is recovered by an infinite dimensional state space description. For numerical implementation the introduced models require for an appropriate approximation. Therefore, a Krylov subspace method and a finite element approach are proposed. The nonlinear lumped parameter models are validated on the basis of experimental data.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the rapid development of electric mobility Li-ion batteries have gained significantly in importance in recent years. Li-ion batteries are characterized by their high energy density and long service life [1]. In order to maximize their lifetime despite increasing charge and discharge rates, sophisticated battery management strategies are required, which rely on precise mathematical models. Since batteries consist of groups of battery cells, the underlying cell models are of particular importance for these battery models. Thereby, battery cell models can be divided into several classes, namely, electro-chemical models, phenomenologically equivalent circuit models (ECMs) [2], and purely data-driven models [3]. Electro-chemical models describe the mass transport and the reaction kinetics of the lithium ions when charging and discharging a battery cell. Such models allow for a deep insight into the physical and chemical phenomena governing the dynamics of the cells. Though they can be highly accurate, their numerical implementation is rather demanding and not suitable for long term-simulations of whole batteries. Moreover, they depend on parameterizations which are not immediate to obtain [2,4,5]. In contrast, purely data-driven models are constructed on the basis of experimental data using machine learning methods without preassuming a particular structure of the model. Such models are able to resemble phenomena that may not yet be sufficiently physically understood. However, as data-driven models are based on measurements only, they cannot easily be generalized to different cell types the dynamics of which may be dominated by different phenomena. Moreover, the quality of such models strongly depends on the available data [5,6]. Finally, ECMs are popular means in order to describe the input–output behavior of battery cells. Thereby, the cell dynamics are described in terms of electrical equivalent circuits. While possessing a predefined structure the particular parameterization of ECMs are obtained from experimental data [5,7–9]. Thus they can be understood as a compromise between the electro-chemical models and the data driven models.

The present contribution follows the latter of the above described approaches. In a battery cell between the electrodes and the electrolyte a double layer is formed. At this double layer electro-chemical charge transfer reactions occur [10,11]. The nonlinear mathematical model describing the relation between the charge transfer and the electro-chemical potential at the double layer is known as the Butler–Volmer equation [9,12–14]. At low temperatures this nonlinearity becomes dominant which leads to an overall nonlinear current–voltage dependency [11,15,16].

The linear (tangential) small-signal behavior of the described nonlinear process is typically measured by a dynamic electrochemical impedance measurements (DEIS) [17]. The impedance at each operating point can be approximated by a linear ECM in terms of fractional transfer functions. [13,14]. Thus, in each operating point the small signal behavior is known. An exact infinite-dimensional state–space realization of this small-signal behavior can be obtained by the so-called diffusive representation of the transfer-functions [18,19]
1
1 Common means for describing fractional input–output behavior in the time domain are the Riemann–Liouville or the Caputo definitions of the fractional derivative by means of an impulse response [20]. However, as this input–output behavior is not exactly reproducible by ordinary differential equations, i.e., by a finite-dimensional dynamical system, an exact state–space realization of fractional transfer functions necessarily requires a description on an infinite-dimensional state space. Such realizations can be obtained using the above-mentioned diffusive representation. Note that there exist popular alternatives for the numerical implementation, which will not be pursued further within the contribution since they rely on a priori approximations of the fractional input–output behavior. Approaches employing the Grünwald–Letnikov fractional derivative [20], which is defined as a limit of generalized difference quotients, require an a priori choice of a step size for the difference quotients and a series truncation. Moreover, this approach does not lead to an approximation of the system dynamics by a finite number of ordinary differential equations, as required for efficient numerical simulation by standard simulation algorithms. Finally, a priori approximation of fractional transfer functions in the frequency domain, as proposed in [21], has proven to be an efficient means for the approximation of linear fractional input–output behavior. As the focus of this contribution is on nonlinear modeling, such approaches will not be pursued further in the following. However, these linear models are not suitable for describing the nonlinear large-signal behavior of the battery cell [9], in particular at low temperatures.

In the field of equivalent circuit models there is a gap in the literature. While physical models can describe the behavior of a battery cell at low temperatures [22], to the best knowledge of the authors, this is not the case for equivalent circuit models. Tough this latter models can describe the input–output behavior of the battery cell at individual operating points by adjusting the parameters [15,23–25], there is still no equivalent circuit model, that can describe the nonlinear large-signal behavior. Therefore, the focus and the main result of this paper is on the reconstruction of the nonlinear large-signal behavior of a battery cell based on equivalent circuit models. To this end two nonlinear models 2
2 The presented nonlinear models extend the models introduced in [26]. are introduced both of which can describe the dynamics of a battery cell even at low temperatures and high discharge currents. These models can be understood as an extension to the linear Randles circuit. 3
3 The Randles circuit is an ECM commonly used to describe the double layer in a battery cell as well as diffusion phenomena [14,27].

The first model, which is referred to as nonlinear equivalent circuit model (nECM) in the sequel, allows for an interpretation in terms of nonlinear circuit elements. The second model, referred to as alternative nonlinear state-space model (nSSM), does not allow for a circuit interpretation but, offers more flexibility. In order to show the efficiency of the proposed methods, the obtained models are validated by means of measurements and compared to a linear ECM, which is equivalent to the Randles circuit. Note that, phenomena like the dependence of the model on temperature and the state of charge (SOC) are not taken into account in this contribution. They may, however, be considered as slowly varying parameters.

The article is structured as follows: Section 3 is devoted to the derivation of the nonlinear models as well as their discretization. In Section 4 the obtained models are compared in simulation studies and validated by means of experimental data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linear cell models at the operating points
ce:section.ce:para:
Experimentally, the dynamic behavior of a battery cell can be determined by the DEIS, i.e., by measuring the frequency response of the cell in several operating points. 4
4 In this article, the term operating point refers to its system-theoretic definition. Thereby, an operating point is an operating mode of the system in which all system variables remain constant in time, i.e. all their derivatives disappear [28]. To this end a prescribed input current is applied to the battery cell, which is composed of a constant operating current and a harmonic current signal with small amplitude. Assuming the cell temperature to be constant and the charging (resp. discharging) process of the battery cell to be significantly slower compared to the small signal frequency this procedure corresponds to a harmonic excitation of the system from a (quasi)-stationary operating point. Measuring the corresponding harmonic voltage response of the system, i.e., the difference between the (quasi)-stationary operating voltage and the measured excitation, the frequency response in the particular operating point corresponds to the quotient of the Fourier transforms of the involved small amplitude signals. Due to the nonlinearity of the underlying physical processes the measured frequency-response essentially depends on the particular operating point.

The Nyquist plots corresponding to the DEIS measurements, the models presented in this article are based on, are shown in Fig. 1a. This correspond to a battery cell with 50 Ah used in the automotive sector. The measurements were done at
− 20
°C
at an SOC of
70 %
in
10
operating points between
i
̄
= 0
A
and
i
̄
= − 100
A
. For the measurements a BaSyTec HPS cell test system was used as a current source, the voltage was measured using a Keithley DMM7510 multimeter, to ensure only small changes in temperature ( ≤ 0.5 K) during the measurement the cell was cooled with an antifreeze water cooling system, the SOC drift was less than 2 % to keep the change in impedance minimal. Further details concerning the measurement setup can be found in [29].

The measurement at
i
̄
= 0
A differs from the remaining ones, since the impedance was recorded down to
0.003
Hz
. Such slow frequencies cannot be recorded at large operating currents without significantly changing the SOC of the cell. Therefore, similar to the results of [9] it is assumed that the impedance at slow frequencies, i.e. less than
0.1
Hz
, can be recovered from the measurements at
i
̄
= 0
A
. The impedance, i.e., the tangential dynamics of the battery cell, in each operating point can be reproduced by the ECM which is shown in Fig. 1b. This ECM consists of three elements: the internal resistance, which causes these semicircles to be shifted on the real axis, the so called RQ-element, 5
5 There are various names in the literature for this transfer function like Zarc- [30], RQ- [31] or Cole–Cole-element [32]. In this article the name RQ-element is used. which approximates the compressed semicircles, and a constant-phase element which represents the slow frequencies. The constant-phase element is a fractional integrator [33] multiplied by a constant, therein, the constant exponent
α
w
can take values between 0 and 1 . The RQ-element consist of a parallel connection of a constant-phase element and an ohmic resistor. The impedance of the this circuit is given by
(1)
G
( s )
s = j ω

R

+
R
1 + R Q
( j ω )

+
1
Q
w
( j ω )

w
,
ω ∈
R
≥ 0
.
As described in the introduction of this contribution the equivalent circuit which is often used to reproduce the impedance measurement of a Li-ion battery cell is the so called Randle’s Circuit [14,27], because of computational advantages in this contribution the circuit from Fig. 1b is used, whereby in [27] it is shown that the difference between the here used circuit and the Randle’s Circuit is negligible. The impedance of the RQ-element
(2)
G
R Q
( s )
s = j ω
=
R
1 + R Q
( j ω )
α
,
is the basis for the nonlinear models introduced in the following. The derivation of these models is achieved in several steps.

Firstly, the ECM from Fig. 1b is parameterized according to the measurements shown in Fig. 1a, where the parameters are determined by fitting Eq. (1) into the DEIS. In the case of the nECM it is assumed that the parameters
α
w
,
Q

and
R
i
as well as the fractional exponent α of the RQ-element are independent of the operating point. Thus, only the remaining two parameters of the RQ-element R and Q depend on the operating point considered. Therefore, the parameters

Above

determines the operating point and
k = 1 , … , n
indicates the frequency at which the impedance was measured. Note that the optimization problem takes into account the entire DEIS. When parameterizing the nSSM, it is assumed that
R

are constant, and coincide with those of the nECM. Therefore, in each operating point the parameters of the RQ-element, thus the triples
q

(4)

;

G

( j

k
)
|
2
,
l = 1 , … , m .
In this case, the impedance at each operating point is considered separately.

Secondly, for each of the operating points a state–space realization of the RQ element, which describes the dynamics of the cell in the time domain, is derived via the so called diffusive representation of the fractional transfer functions [18,19]. The resulting linear models are valid in a neighborhood of the corresponding operating points only.

The nonlinear dynamical models covering a broad range of the operating space are determined by interpolating between the linear models obtained in the previous section in an appropriate way. Due to the infinite-dimensional character of the fractional transfer models, the involved state–space descriptions are infinite dimensional and require for appropriate approximations. The nonlinear models do not represent the full battery dynamics, but replace the RQ element in the linear ECM in Fig. 1b.

The first nonlinear model introduced is the nECM. This model can be interpreted as a nonlinear equivalent circuit possessing the same structure as the RQ element. In the case of the nECM the linear components R and Q are replaced by nonlinear static components while the fractional exponent α is assumed to be constant. The nECM can be approximated by a finite dimensional state–space model by applying the Krylov subspace method [34] to the linear components of the circuit. The second nonlinear model presented below is the nSSM, which interpolates the state–space representations of the RQ element in a more abstract manner. In contrast to the ECM, this approach does not require the assumption that the fractional exponent of α is constant, which offers more flexibility. However, as a drawback the nSSM does not allow for a simple interpretation as the nECM. For the discretization of the nSSM a finite element method is used.

To derive the nECM, a state–space realization of the RQ element (2) is required. For this purpose a state–space realization of a fractional integrator is presented. This realization is then extended to derive a realization of the RQ element. Subsequently, the state of the fractional integrator is denoted by η while its input and output are denoted by
i Q
and q , respectively. The desired state–space realization of the fractional integrator is derived from the diffusive representation of the corresponding transfer function [18,19]:
(5)

The nonlinear state–space realization of the nECM is described by the state x , the system input 6
6 Throughout this article the electrical notational conventions are preferred over the system theoretical ones. As a consequence, all currents are denoted by i even if they refer to the system input usually denoted by u . Moreover, voltages are denoted by u , even if they refer to the system output usually denoted by y .
i , and the system output u . The RQ element corresponds to the tangent approximation of the nECM in each operating point. Therefore, the RQ element, shown in Fig. 1b, is able to describe the behavior of the nonlinear system for small deviations

i

of the RQ element and the current through the resistor. Where the current through the resistor can be described by Ohm’s law

This realization is a linear time domain model corresponding to the measurements shown in Fig. 1 where R and Q depend on the particular operating point. In order to derive the nonlinear model it is assumed that R and Q are known as continuous functions of the current

.
This completes the derivation of the nECM. The result is the nonlinear infinite dimensional state–space model (9) in which the functions f and g are given according to (12). For the purpose of numerical simulations the infinite dimensional model needs to be approximated by a finite dimensional state–space system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Finite dimensional approximation of the nECM
ce:section.ce:para: To discretize the infinite-dimensional nECM (9) the Krylov subspace method [34] is employed. This method allows for a sufficiently accurate approximation of the infinite dimensional system by few ordinary differential equations, only. As the infinite dimensional character of the nECM results from the fractional integrator (7), this circuit element is replaced by a finite dimensional approximation, while the nonlinear characteristics are taken into account in the input and the output of the discretized system.

For the sake of simplicity the semi-infinite domain
σ ∈
[ 0 , ∞ )
of the fractional integrator is restricted to a bounded domain
σ ∈
[ 0 ,
σ
f
]
neglecting the fast behavior of the system. The result of the discretization by the Krylov subspace method is a finite dimensional linear state–space model whose transfer function coincides with the transfer function of the original system at predefined frequencies
s
1
, … ,
s
n
called moments [34]. The restriction of the state–space description (7) of the fractional integrator to the bounded domain
[ 0 ,
σ
f
]
is given by
(13)

= − σ η

= 0 .

∈ X
is defined, onto which (13) is projected. For this projection a second operator
W : X →

is defined as
(16)

The shape functions and the test functions are chosen according to the state–space system (13) as [34]

In order to get the finite dimensional approximation the operator W is applied to (13)

Using the approximation (15) of the state η leads to the finite dimensional approximation

.
Note that, the moments
s
k
cannot be chosen arbitrary but must be part of the resolvent set
s
k
∈ ρ
( A )
of the system operator A . In the presented case
ρ
( A )
= ℂ ∖
[ 0 , −
σ
f
)
. Up to now a finite dimensional approximation of the state–space model (19) has been derived. In order to obtain the finite dimensional description of the complete model, the nonlinear static characteristics (12) must be taken into account, similarly as for the derivation of the nECM in (9). This finally leads to the finite dimension approximation of the nECM given by
(21)
ζ
̇
( t )
=
A
n
ζ
( t )
+
B
n
( i
( t )
−
f
̄
(
C
n

( t )
)
)
,
u = g
(
C
n
ζ
( t )
)
.
With a suitable choice of the moments, the infinite dimensional system can be approximated in the relevant bandwidth very precisely by few equations.

As mentioned in Section 2 the nECM has the disadvantage that the fractional exponent α is a constant. Therefore, an alternative model nSSM is introduced below. The model structure used to determine the nSSM is given by
(22)
z
̇
( σ , t )
= − σ z
( σ , t )
+ h
( σ , i
( t )
)
,
u
( t )
=
∫
0
∞
z
( σ , t )
d σ ,
where z is the state used to describe the nSSM. The input i and the output u possess the same meaning as in the nECM. The nSSM is derived from an alternative state–space realization of the fractional transfer function (2) associated with the following diffusive representation [18,19]:
(23)
G
R Q
( s )
=
R
1 + R Q
s
α
=
∫
0
∞
γ
α , R , Q
( σ )
σ + s
d σ ,
with
σ ∈
[ 0 , ∞ )
and the weighting factor
γ
α , R , Q
( σ )
, given as
(24)
γ
α , R , Q
( σ )
=
R
2
Q sin
( π α )
σ
α
π
(
R
2
Q
2
σ
2 α
+ 2 R Q cos
( π α )
σ

+ 1 )
.
Again, the state–space realization of the transfer function (23) is the linear approximation of the nSSM (22) in an operating point
z
̄
,
i
̄
and
u
̄
. Therefore, for small deviations from the operating point
z
̃
= z −
z
̄
,
i
̃
= i −
i
̄
and
u
̃
= u −
u
̄
the realization is given by
(25)
z
̃
̇
( σ , t )
= − σ
z
̃

+
γ
α , R , Q

̃
( t )
,

̃
( t )
=
∫

∞

̃

d σ .

Similar to the derivation of nECM the parameters
R = R
i
̄
and
Q = Q
i
̄
depend on the operating point
i
̄
= i
. In contrast to the nECM this approach (22) allows the fractional exponent to be a function of the operating point
α = α
i
̄
. Assuming (22) to be an adequate model to describe the large signal behavior, its tangent linearization, given by
(26)
z
̃
̇
( σ , t )
= − σ
z
̃

+
∂ h

∂ i

̄

̃
,
u
̃
=
∫
0
∞
z
̃
( σ , t )
d σ ,
must coincide with (25) in each operating point. Equating like coefficients of this linear state–space system and the model (25) yields
(27)
∂ h
( σ , i )
∂ i
i
̄
=
γ
α
(
i
̄
)
, R
(

̄
)
, Q
(

̄
)
( σ )
.
This equation can be integrated in order to determine the nonlinear input operator
(28)
h
( σ , i )
=
∫
0
i
γ
( α
(
i
̄
)
, R
(
i
̄
)
, Q
(
i
̄
)
, σ )
d
i
̄
,
with
h
( 0 )
= 0
. Thus, the derivation of nSSM is complete. The nSSM (22) is a nonlinear infinite dimensional system. In order to perform simulations the system has to be discretized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Finite dimensional approximation of the nSSM
ce:section.ce:para:
Unlike the nECM, the nSSM does not allow for a separation of the nonlinear part and the infinite dimensional part of the state–space system. This means that linear techniques as the Krylov subspace method are not applicable to the nSSM. Instead, a finite element method is applied in order to discretize the nSSM (22). To this end the semi infinite domain
σ ∈
[ 0 , ∞ )
is restricted to a bounded domain
σ ∈
[ 0 ,
σ
f
]
, similarly as for the discretization of the nECM in Section 3.1.1. Again, this restriction corresponds to the neglection of the fast system behavior. With this simplifications a standard finite element method can be applied to the state–space system (22).

The nonlinear models introduced interpolate between the small signal models, which describe the dynamics for small deviations from an operating point. These models possess structural differences. 8
8 In the derivation of the nECM Section 3.1 it is assumed that the parameter Q depends on the operating voltage
u
̄
. In contrast in the derivation of the nSSM in Section 3.2
Q is a function of the operating current
i
̄
. Note that the voltage
u
̄
and the current
i
̄
in any stationary operating point are related to each other via the bijection
i
̄
= f
(
u
̄
)
and
u
̄
=
f
− 1
(
i
̄
)
(12). Therefore, the nonlinearities of the model are equivalently parameterized by the operating current or by the operating voltage. The different choices have beet taken in order to simplify the notation.

From the derivation of the nonlinear models, it follows that their Taylor series expansion around an operating point coincides in the linear term. In general, the models will differ with respect to the higher order terms. Since no further physical or numerical analysis of the large-signal behavior has been performed so far, at this point it is not possible to decide which of the two models describes the large-signal behavior more accurately.

Nevertheless, the two models can be compared. Structural assumptions were made in the derivation of both models. The nECM is the extension of a linear equivalent circuit where the structure of the nonlinear model coincides with the circuit of the RQ element according to Fig. 1b. The structural assumption associated with the nSSM is explicitly given by (22). When compared to the nSSM the advantage of the nECM consists in its interpretability as a circuit. Moreover, the nECM can be easily approximated by a finite dimensional system by means of linear model order reduction techniques, as the Krylov subspace method. An appropriate choice of moments leads to a precise discretization with few equations. In contrast such linear reduction schemes are not applicable to the nSSM. Therefore, the system is discretized according to the finite element method. As a consequence the obtained finite-dimensional state–space model is of higher order when compared to nECM. While the linear representation of the nECM in an operating point depends on the parameter R and Q , the fractional exponent α is assumed to be constant regardless of the operating point. In contrast for the nSSM this parameter is explicitly allowed to depend on the operating point. Thus, the nSSM is more flexible than the nECM. In the following section, both models are parameterized by experimental data and compared by means of simulations and experiments.

In this section the linear equivalent circuit from Fig. 1b and the two nonlinear battery models are mutually compared. The latter models possess the same topology as the linear model. Remember that in the nonlinear models the RQ element is either replaced by the nSSM or by the nECM.

For the sake of completeness, the infinite-dimensional state–space representations of the three battery models are presented below, whereby the fractional integrator in all models is described according to (7). The linear battery model, which is the realization of (1), is given by
η
̇
w
( σ , t )
= − σ
η
w
( σ , t )
+
1
Q
w
i
( t )
(29)
x
̇
0

= − σ
x

+ i
( t )

1

0

0

∞

d σ ,
σ , t ∈
R
≥ 0

=
∫

∞

w
( σ )
η

( σ , t )
+
1
Q
0

0
( σ )

0

d σ +
R
i
i
( t )
,
where for the RQ element the realization (8) is used. The nonlinear battery model which includes the nECM is given by
η
̇
w
( σ , t )
= − σ
η
w
( σ , t )
+
1
Q
w
i
( t )

̇

= − σ x

+ i
( t )
−
f
̄
( q
( t )
)
,
σ , t ∈
R
≥ 0
(30)
q
( t )
=
∫

∞

( σ )

d σ

( t )
=
∫

∞

α
w
( σ )
η
w
( σ , t )
d σ + g
( q
( t )
)
+
R
i
i
( t )
.

Finally, the nonlinear battery model which includes the nSSM can be described by
η
̇
w
( σ , t )
= − σ
η
w
( σ , t )
+
1
Q
w
i
( t )
(31)
z
̇
( σ , t )
= − σ z
( σ , t )
+ h
( σ , i
( t )
)
,
σ , t ∈
R
≥ 0
u
( t )
=
∫
0
∞
μ
α
w
( σ )
η
w
( σ , t )
+ z
( σ , t )
d σ +
R
i
i
( t )
.

All parameters have been determined according to Section 2. The result of the fitting are the green curves shown in Fig. 2a, which almost coincide with the measurements. All parameters that do not depend on a particular operating point as well as the parameters of the linear battery model
R
0
,
Q
0
, and
α
0
in (29) are determined while parameterizing the nECM, which means by minimizing (3).

The numerical values for the parameters that do not depend on a particular operating point are
R
i
= 1.62
mΩ
,
α
w
= 0.7
and
Q
w
= 5577
As
α
w
V
− 1
. The numerical values of the parameters of the nECM are shown in Fig. 2d, whereby the fractional exponent is
α = 0.74
. In order to derive the characteristics (12) from the parameters, they are interpolated in between the operating points. Finally, the parameters of the linear battery model (29) correspond to the parameters at the operating point
i
̄
= 0
A, thus
R
0
= 11
mΩ
,
Q
0
= 16 . 31
As
α
0
V
− 1
and
α
0
= 0 . 74
.

For the parameterization of the nSSM the already known parameters
R
i
,
α
w
and
Q
w
are taken into account. The remaining parameters shown in Fig. 2c are determined with respect to the cost function (4). In between the operating points, the values of the parameters are interpolated.

For numerical simulations the three infinite dimensional battery model have been approximated by finite dimensional state–space systems. For all three models the supremum of the domain, according to Sections 3.1.1 and 3.2.1, is
σ
f
= 1
0
5
. The linear battery model (29) as well as the (30) are discretized by means of the Krylov subspace method. In both models the constant-phase element is approximated by
12
ordinary differential equations where the moments
s
1
, …
s
12
are chosen logarithmically in between
s
1
= 1
0
− 4
and
s
12
= 1
0
4
. Additionally, the RQ element in (29), the nECM in (30) and the constant-phase element in (31) are approximated in the same way. The nSSM in model (31) is approximated by means of the finite element method using
40
ordinary differential equations. For this purpose the domain
σ ∈
[ 0 ,
σ
f
]
is discretized using
40
grid points
σ
1
, …
σ
40
. The
39
positive grid points are distributed logarithmically from
σ
2
= 1
0
− 6
to
σ
40
=
σ
f
while
σ
1
= 0
.

For simplicity, no new names for the battery models are introduced, therefore in the following nSSM refers to the entire battery model (31), similarly nECM refers to (30) and LM refers to the linear battery model (29).

The impedance of the discretized nonlinear battery models (31) and (30) is determined in simulation studies to validate the approximation quality as well as the small signal behavior of the models. The results of the simulations are the red curves in Fig. 2b. These red curves match the impedance measurements in each operating point nearly exactly. Thus, it is shown that the small signal behavior of the nonlinear battery models in the operating points is as expected. Additional, the discretization quality is proven. Hardly any differences between the two models can be detected on the basis of the small-signal behavior. This is due to the fact that the parameterization works very well for both models, which can be seen in the figure Fig. 2a. The fitted green curves reproduce the measurements very accurately in both cases.

The large signal behavior of the battery models has been studied in measurements. To this end, the input–output behavior of the battery cell is measured using a dynamic current profile as input. The resulting output voltages are compared with the simulations of the battery models. Two different experiments have been conducted, the first one at an SOC of
70
% and the second one at
90
% SOC. In both cases four current profiles are used to validate the models. The first two current profiles consist in a DC current of
− 15
A
superposed by a sinusoidal AC current with an amplitude of
10
A
and frequencies of
1
Hz
and
10
Hz
, respectively. In a second experiment, a larger excitation current has been considered with a DC current of
− 60
A
superposed by a sinusoidal AC current with an amplitude of
55
A
and frequencies of
1
Hz
and
10
Hz
.

The results of the first experiments corresponding to a DC current of
− 15
A
are presented in Figs. 3a and 3d. The figures show that both nonlinear models are very precise with a maximal error of
ε = 0 . 3
%
. Furthermore, it can be seen that even with this small excitation, the linear model cannot adequately reproduce the measurements. Figs. 3c and 3d show that the simulation results of the nonlinear battery models accurately resemble the measurements also in the case of a DC excitation of
− 60
A
, while the linear model cannot reproduce these data. The maximal error between the simulations of the nonlinear models and measurements is about
ε = 1
%
.

The purpose of this experiment has been to emphasize the fact that the models can describe the dynamics of the battery cell precisely in a broad SOC range. Since the impedance depends on the SOC it has been assumed that the simulation results are less accurate in comparison to the case of
70
% SOC. However, the experiment at the DC excitation current of
− 15
A
, depicted in Figs. 4a and 4b, shows that both models describe the measurements accurately. The same applies to the experiments at a DC excitation current of
− 60
A
shown in Figs. 4c and 4d. The increase in error ε is barely visible comparing the results of this experiments to those at
70
% SOC. This shows that the models can also predict the behavior of the battery cell at different SOC. Note that even an explicit identification of the parameters at
70
% SOC did not yield more precise results.

In this contribution, two methods for modeling the nonlinear dynamics of a Li-ion battery cell even at low temperatures based on impedance measurements are presented. Starting from the linear description of the impedance data two different state–space realizations are proposed. Both models are infinite dimensional. Therefore, an appropriate approximation scheme is presented for each of the proposed models. Finally, the validation of the numerical simulation of the models against the measurements show the accuracy and the efficiency of the proposed models.

According to the dataset used in this paper, the nECM is preferable to the nSSM because it is interpretable as an equivalent circuit and it can be discretized according to the Krylov method. The additional flexibility offered by the nSSM is not required to describe the considered dataset. Nevertheless, this is not a universal statement, since the DEIS depends on the SOC and temperature. Therefore, when considering a different SOC or temperature, the greater flexibility could be useful. Nevertheless, it was clearly shown that the models presented perform significantly better than the linear battery model which is often used in the literature.

Using the presented models the electrical behavior of a battery cell can be simulated. Currently, all the measurements have been taken at constant temperatures and constant SOC. Therefore, the dependence of the model on these variables has not been explicitly taken into account. As these quantities will indeed change in real applications, it would be of big interest to integrate the thermal behavior of the battery cell as well as the dynamic dependency on the SOC into an extended model. However, since the presented approaches are very flexible, the extension can be easily implemented. Moreover, though in the current setup the linear ansatz
1
Q
w
s
α
w
for the Warburg impedance gave excellent results, future research will investigate whether and under what conditions this impedance should be replaced by a nonlinear circuit element. It should be noted that such an analysis cannot be performed by means of impedance spectroscopy as the SOC can no longer be assumed to be constant at the low frequencies and high operating currents required.

Lukas Tappeiner: Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Simulation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Marc Oldenburger: Data curation, Conceptualization, Methodology. Benjamin Bedürftig: Data curation, Conceptualization, Methodology. Frank Woittennek: Project administration, Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Renew. Sustain. Energy Rev.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Characterization of high-power lithium-ion batteries by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. I. Experimental investigation
